Create clean light-mode presentations with spacious typography, simple sections, charts, and imagery.

$openai-templates:artifact-template-stra.name: $openai-templates:artifact-template-strategy-memorandum
$openai-templates:artifact-template-stra.description: Create a document using the Strategy Memorandum template and its retained reference file. Use when the user selects or names Strategy Memorandum. Present strategic context, choices, rationale, risks, milestones, and a clear recommendation.

$openai-templates:artifact-template-syst.name: $openai-templates:artifact-template-system-design
$openai-templates:artifact-template-syst.description: Create a document using the System Design template and its retained reference file. Use when the user selects or names System Design. Document system architecture, requirements, components, data flows, APIs, tradeoffs, and operational considerations.

$openai-templates:artifact-template-team.name: $openai-templates:artifact-template-team-alignment
$openai-templates:artifact-template-team.description: Create a presentation using the Team Alignment template and its retained reference file. Use when the user selects or names Team Alignment. Facilitate team offsites and planning with context, goals, priorities, decisions, and action items.

$openai-templates:artifact-template-thre.name: $openai-templates:artifact-template-three-statement-forecast
$openai-templates:artifact-template-thre.description: Create a spreadsheet using the Three-Statement Forecast template and its retained reference file. Use when the user selects or names Three-Statement Forecast. Build an integrated income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow forecast with assumptions, checks, and an executive summary.

$pdf.name: $pdf
$pdf.description: Use when tasks involve reading, creating, or reviewing PDF files where rendering and layout matter; prefer visual checks by rendering pages (Poppler) and use Python tools such as `reportlab`, `pdfplumber`, and `pypdf` for generation and extraction.

$pdf:pdf.name: $pdf:pdf
$pdf:pdf.description: Read, create, inspect, render, and verify PDF files where visual layout matters. Use Poppler rendering plus Python tools such as reportlab, pdfplumber, and pypdf for generation and extraction.

$pm-ai-shipping:intended-vs-implemented.name: $pm-ai-shipping:intended-vs-implemented
$pm-ai-shipping:intended-vs-implemented.description: The method for finding the gap between what a system is supposed to do and what the code actually does — the class of bug generic scanners miss because they have no model of intent. Defines what counts as documented intent, what counts as implementation evidence, which mismatches matter, and how to avoid hand-wavy findings. Use when auditing AI-built code, reviewing access control against documented permissions, or checking whether a codebase matches its own documentation.

$pm-ai-shipping:shipping-artifacts.name: $pm-ai-shipping:shipping-artifacts
$pm-ai-shipping:shipping-artifacts.description: The durable documentation set that makes an AI-built (vibe-coded) app reviewable before shipping. A small core every app needs — architecture, user/permission flows, permissions, variables/secrets, and a test-coverage map — plus conditional docs added only when they apply: emails, scheduled work, SEO, and embedded agents/automation. Defines what each doc must capture and how a reviewer or auditor uses it. Use when documenting a codebase for handoff, mapping user journeys and trust-boundary crossings, planning test coverage, or preparing for a security or performance audit.

$pm-execution:brainstorm-okrs.name: $pm-execution:brainstorm-okrs
$pm-execution:brainstorm-okrs.description: Brainstorm team-level OKRs aligned with company objectives — qualitative objectives with measurable key results. Use when setting quarterly OKRs, aligning team goals with company strategy, drafting objectives, or learning how to write effective OKRs.

$pm-execution:create-prd.name: $pm-execution:create-prd
$pm-execution:create-prd.description: Create a Product Requirements Document using a comprehensive 8-section template covering problem, objectives, segments, value propositions, solution, and release planning. Use when writing a PRD, documenting product requirements, preparing a feature spec, or reviewing an existing PRD.

$pm-execution:dummy-dataset.name: $pm-execution:dummy-dataset
$pm-execution:dummy-dataset.description: Generate realistic dummy datasets for testing with customizable columns, constraints, and output formats (CSV, JSON, SQL, Python script). Use when creating test data, building mock datasets, or generating sample data for development and demos.

$pm-execution:job-stories.name: $pm-execution:job-stories
$pm-execution:job-stories.description: Create job stories using the 'When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [outcome]' format with detailed acceptance criteria. Use when writing job stories, creating JTBD-style backlog items, or expressing user situations and motivations.

$pm-execution:outcome-roadmap.name: $pm-execution:outcome-roadmap
$pm-execution:outcome-roadmap.description: Transform an output-focused roadmap into an outcome-focused one that communicates strategic intent. Rewrites initiatives as outcome statements reflecting user and business impacts. Use when shifting to outcome roadmaps, making a roadmap more strategic, or rewriting feature lists as outcomes.

$pm-execution:pre-mortem.name: $pm-execution:pre-mortem
$pm-execution:pre-mortem.description: Run a pre-mortem risk analysis on a PRD or launch plan. Categorizes risks as Tigers (real problems), Paper Tigers (overblown concerns), and Elephants (unspoken worries), then classifies as launch-blocking, fast-follow, or track. Use when preparing for launch, stress-testing a product plan, or identifying what could go wrong.

$pm-execution:prioritization-frameworks.name: $pm-execution:prioritization-frameworks
$pm-execution:prioritization-frameworks.description: Reference guide to 9 prioritization frameworks with formulas, when-to-use guidance, and templates — RICE, ICE, Kano, MoSCoW, Opportunity Score, and more. Use when selecting a prioritization method, comparing frameworks like RICE vs ICE, or learning how different prioritization approaches work.

$pm-execution:release-notes.name: $pm-execution:release-notes
$pm-execution:release-notes.description: Generate user-facing release notes from tickets, PRDs, or changelogs. Creates clear, engaging summaries organized by category (new features, improvements, fixes). Use when writing release notes, creating changelogs, announcing product updates, or summarizing what shipped.

$pm-execution:retro.name: $pm-execution:retro
$pm-execution:retro.description: Facilitate a structured sprint retrospective — what went well, what didn't, and prioritized action items with owners and deadlines. Use when running a retrospective, reflecting on a sprint, creating action items from team feedback, or learning how to run effective retros.

$pm-execution:sprint-plan.name: $pm-execution:sprint-plan
$pm-execution:sprint-plan.description: Plan a sprint with capacity estimation, story selection, dependency mapping, and risk identification. Use when preparing for sprint planning, estimating team capacity, selecting stories, or balancing sprint scope against velocity.

$pm-execution:stakeholder-map.name: $pm-execution:stakeholder-map
$pm-execution:stakeholder-map.description: Build a stakeholder map using a power/interest grid, identify communication strategies per quadrant, and generate a communication plan. Use when managing stakeholders, preparing for a launch, aligning cross-functional teams, or planning stakeholder engagement.

$pm-execution:strategy-red-team.name: $pm-execution:strategy-red-team
$pm-execution:strategy-red-team.description: Red-team a PRD, roadmap, or strategy by attacking its load-bearing assumptions before reality does. Steelmans then attacks each claim, ranks failure modes by impact × likelihood × cheapness-to-test, and returns the cheapest test and kill criteria for each. Use when stress-testing a plan, pressure-testing a strategy, challenging assumptions, or preparing a doc for executive review.

$pm-execution:summarize-meeting.name: $pm-execution:summarize-meeting
$pm-execution:summarize-meeting.description: Summarize a meeting transcript into structured notes with date, participants, topic, key decisions, summary points, and action items. Use when processing meeting recordings, creating meeting notes, writing meeting minutes, or recapping discussions.

$pm-execution:test-scenarios.name: $pm-execution:test-scenarios
$pm-execution:test-scenarios.description: Create comprehensive test scenarios from user stories with test objectives, starting conditions, user roles, step-by-step actions, and expected outcomes. Use when writing QA test cases, creating test plans, defining acceptance tests, or preparing for feature validation.

$pm-execution:user-stories.name: $pm-execution:user-stories
$pm-execution:user-stories.description: Create user stories following the 3 C's (Card, Conversation, Confirmation) and INVEST criteria with descriptions, design links, and acceptance criteria. Use when writing user stories, breaking down features into backlog items, or defining acceptance criteria.

$pm-execution:wwas.name: $pm-execution:wwas
$pm-execution:wwas.description: Create product backlog items in Why-What-Acceptance format — independent, valuable, testable items with strategic context. Use when writing structured backlog items, breaking features into work items, or using the WWA format.

$pm-go-to-market:beachhead-segment.name: $pm-go-to-market:beachhead-segment
$pm-go-to-market:beachhead-segment.description: Identify the first beachhead market segment for a product launch. Evaluates segments against burning pain, willingness to pay, winnable market share, and referral potential. Use when choosing a first market, targeting an initial customer segment, or planning market entry strategy.

$pm-go-to-market:competitive-battlecard.name: $pm-go-to-market:competitive-battlecard
$pm-go-to-market:competitive-battlecard.description: Create sales-ready competitive battlecards comparing your product against a specific competitor — positioning, feature comparison, objection handling, and win/loss patterns. Use when preparing sales teams, creating competitive materials, or responding to 'why not competitor X?'

$pm-go-to-market:growth-loops.name: $pm-go-to-market:growth-loops
$pm-go-to-market:growth-loops.description: Identify growth loops (flywheels) for sustainable traction. Evaluates 5 loop types: Viral, Usage, Collaboration, User-Generated, and Referral. Use when designing growth mechanisms, building product-led traction, or understanding how growth loops work.

$pm-go-to-market:gtm-motions.name: $pm-go-to-market:gtm-motions
$pm-go-to-market:gtm-motions.description: Identify the best GTM motions and tools across 7 motion types: Inbound, Outbound, Paid Digital, Community, Partners, ABM, and PLG. Use when selecting marketing channels, choosing between inbound and outbound strategy, or planning cross-channel campaigns.

$pm-go-to-market:gtm-strategy.name: $pm-go-to-market:gtm-strategy
$pm-go-to-market:gtm-strategy.description: Create a go-to-market strategy covering marketing channels, messaging, success metrics, and launch timeline. Use when planning a product launch, creating a GTM plan from scratch, or defining a launch strategy for a new market.

$pm-go-to-market:ideal-customer-profile.name: $pm-go-to-market:ideal-customer-profile
$pm-go-to-market:ideal-customer-profile.description: Identify the Ideal Customer Profile (ICP) from research data with demographics, behaviors, JTBD, and needs. Use when defining your ICP, analyzing PMF survey data, or understanding who your best customers are.

$pm-marketing-growth:marketing-ideas.name: $pm-marketing-growth:marketing-ideas
$pm-marketing-growth:marketing-ideas.description: Generate 5 creative, cost-effective marketing ideas with channels, messaging, and engagement rationale. Use when brainstorming marketing campaigns, planning product promotion, or looking for creative marketing tactics.

$pm-marketing-growth:north-star-metric.name: $pm-marketing-growth:north-star-metric
$pm-marketing-growth:north-star-metric.description: Define a North Star Metric and 3-5 supporting input metrics that form a metrics constellation. Classify the business game (Attention, Transaction, Productivity) and validate against 7 criteria for an effective North Star. Use when choosing a North Star Metric, setting up a metrics framework, learning about the North Star Framework, or deciding what to measure.

$pm-marketing-growth:positioning-ideas.name: $pm-marketing-growth:positioning-ideas
$pm-marketing-growth:positioning-ideas.description: Brainstorm product positioning ideas differentiated from competitors. Identifies top competitors and generates positioning statements with rationale. Use when developing product positioning, differentiating from competitors, or crafting brand positioning strategy.

$pm-marketing-growth:product-name.name: $pm-marketing-growth:product-name
$pm-marketing-growth:product-name.description: Brainstorm 5 unique, memorable product names with rationale aligned to brand values and target audience. Use when naming a new product, rebranding, or exploring product name ideas.

$pm-marketing-growth:value-prop-statemen.name: $pm-marketing-growth:value-prop-statements
$pm-marketing-growth:value-prop-statemen.description: Generate value proposition statements for marketing, sales, and onboarding from existing value propositions. Use when writing marketing copy, creating sales messaging, or crafting onboarding messages.

$pm-product-discovery:analyze-feature-re.name: $pm-product-discovery:analyze-feature-requests
$pm-product-discovery:analyze-feature-re.description: Analyze and prioritize a list of feature requests by theme, strategic alignment, impact, effort, and risk. Use when reviewing customer feature requests, triaging a backlog, or making prioritization decisions.

$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-experim.name: $pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-experiments-existing
$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-experim.description: Design experiments to test assumptions for an existing product — prototypes, A/B tests, spikes, and other low-effort validation methods. Use when validating assumptions, testing feature ideas cheaply, or planning product experiments.

$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-experim.name: $pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-experiments-new
$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-experim.description: Design lean startup experiments (pretotypes) for a new product. Creates XYZ hypotheses and suggests low-effort validation methods like landing pages, explainer videos, and pre-orders. Use when validating a new product idea, creating pretotypes, or testing market demand.

$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-ideas-e.name: $pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-ideas-existing
$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-ideas-e.description: Brainstorm product ideas for an existing product using multi-perspective ideation from PM, Designer, and Engineer viewpoints. Use when generating new feature ideas, brainstorming solutions for an identified opportunity, or ideating with a product trio.

$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-ideas-n.name: $pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-ideas-new
$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-ideas-n.description: Brainstorm feature ideas for a new product in initial discovery from PM, Designer, and Engineer perspectives. Use when starting product discovery for a new product, exploring features for a startup idea, or doing initial ideation.

$pm-product-discovery:identify-assumptio.name: $pm-product-discovery:identify-assumptions-existing
$pm-product-discovery:identify-assumptio.description: Identify risky assumptions for a feature idea in an existing product across Value, Usability, Viability, and Feasibility. Uses multi-perspective devil's advocate thinking. Use when stress-testing a feature idea, doing risk assessment, or preparing for assumption mapping.

$pm-product-discovery:identify-assumptio.name: $pm-product-discovery:identify-assumptions-new
$pm-product-discovery:identify-assumptio.description: Identify risky assumptions for a new product idea across 8 risk categories including Go-to-Market, Strategy, and Team. Use when evaluating startup risks, assessing a new product concept, or mapping assumptions for a new venture.

$pm-product-discovery:interview-script.name: $pm-product-discovery:interview-script
$pm-product-discovery:interview-script.description: Create a structured customer interview script with JTBD probing questions, warm-up, core exploration, and wrap-up sections. Follows The Mom Test principles — no leading questions, no pitching, focus on past behavior. Use when preparing for user interviews, creating interview guides, or planning discovery research.

$pm-product-discovery:metrics-dashboard.name: $pm-product-discovery:metrics-dashboard
$pm-product-discovery:metrics-dashboard.description: Define and design a product metrics dashboard with key metrics, data sources, visualization types, and alert thresholds. Use when creating a metrics dashboard, defining KPIs, setting up product analytics, or building a data monitoring plan.

$pm-product-discovery:opportunity-soluti.name: $pm-product-discovery:opportunity-solution-tree
$pm-product-discovery:opportunity-soluti.description: Build an Opportunity Solution Tree (OST) to structure product discovery — map a desired outcome to opportunities, solutions, and experiments. Based on Teresa Torres' Continuous Discovery Habits. Use when structuring discovery work, mapping opportunities to solutions, or deciding what to build next.

$pm-product-discovery:prioritize-assumpt.name: $pm-product-discovery:prioritize-assumptions
$pm-product-discovery:prioritize-assumpt.description: Prioritize assumptions using an Impact × Risk matrix and suggest experiments for each. Use when triaging a list of assumptions, deciding what to test first, or applying the assumption prioritization canvas.

$pm-product-discovery:prioritize-feature.name: $pm-product-discovery:prioritize-features
$pm-product-discovery:prioritize-feature.description: Prioritize a backlog of feature ideas based on impact, effort, risk, and strategic alignment with top 5 recommendations. Use when prioritizing a feature backlog, making scope decisions, or ranking product ideas.

$pm-product-discovery:summarize-intervie.name: $pm-product-discovery:summarize-interview
$pm-product-discovery:summarize-intervie.description: Summarize a customer interview transcript into a structured template with JTBD, satisfaction signals, and action items. Use when processing interview recordings or transcripts, synthesizing discovery interviews, or creating interview summaries.

$pm-product-strategy:ansoff-matrix.name: $pm-product-strategy:ansoff-matrix
$pm-product-strategy:ansoff-matrix.description: Generate an Ansoff Matrix analysis mapping growth strategies across market penetration, market development, product development, and diversification. Use when considering growth options, planning market expansion, or evaluating strategic growth paths.

$pm-product-strategy:business-model.name: $pm-product-strategy:business-model
$pm-product-strategy:business-model.description: Generate a Business Model Canvas with all 9 building blocks. Use when creating a business model, documenting how a business creates value, or analyzing an existing business model.

$pm-product-strategy:lean-canvas.name: $pm-product-strategy:lean-canvas
$pm-product-strategy:lean-canvas.description: Generate a Lean Canvas with problem, solution, metrics, cost structure, UVP, unfair advantage, channels, segments, and revenue. Use when exploring a lean startup canvas, testing a business hypothesis, or modeling a new venture.

$pm-product-strategy:monetization-strate.name: $pm-product-strategy:monetization-strategy
$pm-product-strategy:monetization-strate.description: Brainstorm 3-5 monetization strategies with audience fit, risks, and validation experiments. Use when exploring revenue models, evaluating pricing strategies, or deciding how to monetize a product.

$pm-product-strategy:pestle-analysis.name: $pm-product-strategy:pestle-analysis
$pm-product-strategy:pestle-analysis.description: Perform a PESTLE analysis covering Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, and Environmental factors. Use when assessing the macro environment, doing strategic planning, or evaluating external factors affecting your business.

$pm-product-strategy:porters-five-forces.name: $pm-product-strategy:porters-five-forces
$pm-product-strategy:porters-five-forces.description: Perform Porter's Five Forces analysis — competitive rivalry, supplier power, buyer power, threat of substitutes, and threat of new entrants. Use when analyzing industry dynamics, assessing competitive forces, or evaluating market attractiveness.

$pm-product-strategy:pricing-strategy.name: $pm-product-strategy:pricing-strategy
$pm-product-strategy:pricing-strategy.description: Analyze and design pricing strategies including pricing models, competitive pricing analysis, willingness-to-pay estimation, and price elasticity. Use when setting prices, evaluating pricing models, preparing for a pricing change, or comparing freemium vs paid approaches.

$pm-product-strategy:product-strategy.name: $pm-product-strategy:product-strategy
$pm-product-strategy:product-strategy.description: Create a comprehensive product strategy using the 9-section Product Strategy Canvas — vision, segments, costs, value propositions, trade-offs, metrics, growth, capabilities, and defensibility. Use when building a product strategy, creating a strategic plan, or defining product direction.

$pm-product-strategy:product-vision.name: $pm-product-strategy:product-vision
$pm-product-strategy:product-vision.description: Brainstorm an inspiring, achievable, and emotional product vision that motivates teams and aligns stakeholders. Use when defining or refining a product vision, creating a vision statement, or aligning the team around a shared direction.

$pm-product-strategy:startup-canvas.name: $pm-product-strategy:startup-canvas
$pm-product-strategy:startup-canvas.description: Generate a Startup Canvas combining Product Strategy (9 sections) and Business Model (costs + revenue) for a new product. An alternative to BMC and Lean Canvas that separates strategy from business model. Use when launching a new product or evaluating a startup concept.

$pm-product-strategy:swot-analysis.name: $pm-product-strategy:swot-analysis
$pm-product-strategy:swot-analysis.description: Perform a detailed SWOT analysis — strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats with actionable recommendations. Use when doing strategic assessment, competitive analysis, or evaluating a product or business position.

$pm-product-strategy:value-proposition.name: $pm-product-strategy:value-proposition
$pm-product-strategy:value-proposition.description: Design a detailed value proposition using a 6-part JTBD template — Who, Why, What before, How, What after, Alternatives. Use when creating a value proposition, analyzing customer value delivery, or articulating why customers should choose your product.

$presentations:Presentations.name: $presentations:Presentations
$presentations:Presentations.description: Create or edit PowerPoint or Google Slides decks

$product-design:audit.name: $product-design:audit
$product-design:audit.description: Audit or critique a product flow, journey, workflow, funnel, onboarding path, checkout path, settings path, screen, or multi-step product experience by capturing screenshots first, then reporting UX, design, and accessibility findings inline from that evidence. Use Figma only when the user explicitly asks for a board. Use when the user asks to audit, review, critique, inspect, assess, analyze, evaluate, or give feedback on a product experience.

$product-design:design-qa.name: $product-design:design-qa
$product-design:design-qa.description: Internal prototype QA helper. Use only after a Product Design prototype, URL-to-code build, or image-to-code build has a source visual target and a rendered implementation to compare before handoff. Do not use for broad UX critique, design critique, product audits, or flow reviews; route those user-facing requests to audit.

$product-design:get-context.name: $product-design:get-context
$product-design:get-context.description: Mandatory design-brief gate for clarifying the product and outcome. Use before ideation, image-to-code builds, redesigns, or product UI work to clarify missing product information and play back the brief before proceeding.

$product-design:ideate.name: $product-design:ideate
$product-design:ideate.description: Generate image-based alternatives, remixes, or new design directions from a Product Design brief. Use when the user asks for design variants, visual exploration, remixes, or image-generated approaches from provided context.

$product-design:image-to-code.name: $product-design:image-to-code
$product-design:image-to-code.description: Implement a selected image, screenshot, mockup, or Image Gen reference as a faithful, responsive frontend.

$product-design:index.name: $product-design:index
$product-design:index.description: Use when Product Design is explicitly invoked, or when the user's main goal is to explore a design, research UX, audit or critique a flow, faithfully clone a visual source, check a built design, or share a prototype. Do not use Product Design for ordinary implementation unless the user explicitly asks for it.

$product-design:research.name: $product-design:research
$product-design:research.description: Run fast, source-grounded UX research on the highest-signal problems users are experiencing with a user-specified digital product. Use when the user asks to research user pain, UX friction, onboarding issues, docs/help problems, developer experience friction, support pain, product workflow issues, or current user complaints for a named product.

$product-design:share.name: $product-design:share
$product-design:share.description: Share a runnable prototype using the user's preferred deployment tool.

$product-design:url-to-code.name: $product-design:url-to-code
$product-design:url-to-code.description: Clone a live URL as a runnable frontend-only local app.

$product-design:user-context.name: $product-design:user-context
$product-design:user-context.description: Load or manage Product Design's saved user context. Use when the user asks to set up Product Design, get started, onboard, save product or design sources, see what Product Design remembers, update saved context, or remember Product Design preferences. Examples include product URLs, Figma files, screenshots, reference images, codebase paths, Storybook, tokens, design systems, brand assets, and general product/design notes.

$sites:sites-building.name: $sites:sites-building
$sites:sites-building.description: Use Sites to build websites, including landing pages, portfolios, dashboards, portals, trackers, hubs, and internal tools. Always use Sites when the project contains `.openai/hosting.json`.

$sites:sites-hosting.name: $sites:sites-hosting
$sites:sites-hosting.description: Host websites with Sites. Always use after `sites-building`, and use for website publishing, deployment, hosting management, or projects containing `.openai/hosting.json`.

$spreadsheets:Spreadsheets.name: $spreadsheets:Spreadsheets
$spreadsheets:Spreadsheets.description: Use this skill when a user requests to create, modify, analyze, visualize, or work with spreadsheet files (`.xlsx`, `.xls`, `.csv`, `.tsv`) or Google Sheets-targeted spreadsheet artifacts with formulas, formatting, charts, tables, and recalculation.

$superpowers:brainstorming.name: $superpowers:brainstorming
$superpowers:brainstorming.description: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.

$superpowers:dispatching-parallel-agents.name: $superpowers:dispatching-parallel-agents
$superpowers:dispatching-parallel-agents.description: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies

$superpowers:executing-plans.name: $superpowers:executing-plans
$superpowers:executing-plans.description: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints

$superpowers:finishing-a-development-bra.name: $superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch
$superpowers:finishing-a-development-bra.description: Use when implementation is complete, all tests pass, and you need to decide how to integrate the work - guides completion of development work by presenting structured options for merge, PR, or cleanup

$superpowers:receiving-code-review.name: $superpowers:receiving-code-review
$superpowers:receiving-code-review.description: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation

$superpowers:requesting-code-review.name: $superpowers:requesting-code-review
$superpowers:requesting-code-review.description: Use when completing tasks, implementing major features, or before merging to verify work meets requirements

$superpowers:subagent-driven-development.name: $superpowers:subagent-driven-development
$superpowers:subagent-driven-development.description: Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session

$superpowers:systematic-debugging.name: $superpowers:systematic-debugging
$superpowers:systematic-debugging.description: Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes

$superpowers:test-driven-development.name: $superpowers:test-driven-development
$superpowers:test-driven-development.description: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code

$superpowers:using-git-worktrees.name: $superpowers:using-git-worktrees
$superpowers:using-git-worktrees.description: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - ensures an isolated workspace exists via native tools or git worktree fallback

$superpowers:using-superpowers.name: $superpowers:using-superpowers
$superpowers:using-superpowers.description: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions

$superpowers:verification-before-complet.name: $superpowers:verification-before-completion
$superpowers:verification-before-complet.description: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always

$superpowers:writing-plans.name: $superpowers:writing-plans
$superpowers:writing-plans.description: Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code

$superpowers:writing-skills.name: $superpowers:writing-skills
$superpowers:writing-skills.description: Use when creating new skills, editing existing skills, or verifying skills work before deployment

$template-creator:template-creator.name: $template-creator:template-creator
$template-creator:template-creator.description: Create or update a reusable personal Codex artifact-template skill. Use when the user invokes $template-creator or asks in natural language to create a template using, from, or based on an attached Word document, PowerPoint presentation, or Excel workbook, or explicitly asks to edit or update a passed artifact-template skill. Do not use for one-off artifact creation from an existing template.

$ui-reference-to-code.name: $ui-reference-to-code
$ui-reference-to-code.description: Recreate UI reference images, screenshots, Figma exports, prototype renders, dashboards, web pages, or mobile screens as high-fidelity maintainable frontend code. Use when asked to turn a UI design or effect image into code, reproduce a screen nearly 1:1, extract only the visual assets that code cannot reliably rebuild, implement the page in an existing or new frontend target, and verify fidelity with real browser screenshots.

$visualize:visualize.name: $visualize:visualize
$visualize:visualize.description: Create visualizations and interactive tools in conversation. Use when asked to show how something works, make simulators or labs, maps, plots, charts or graphs, comparisons, scenarios, adjustable inputs, and exploration.

$imagegen.name: $imagegen
$imagegen.description: Generate or edit raster images when the task benefits from AI-created bitmap visuals such as photos, illustrations, textures, sprites, mockups, or transparent-background cutouts. Use when Codex should create a brand-new image, transform an existing image, or derive visual variants from references, and the output should be a bitmap asset rather than repo-native code or vector. Do not use when the task is better handled by editing existing SVG/vector/code-native assets, extending an established icon or logo system, or building the visual directly in HTML/CSS/canvas.

$openai-docs.name: $openai-docs
$openai-docs.description: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs, asks about Codex itself or choosing Codex surfaces, needs up-to-date official documentation with citations, help choosing the latest model for a use case, or model upgrade and prompt-upgrade guidance; use OpenAI docs MCP tools for non-Codex docs questions, use the Codex manual helper first for broad Codex self-knowledge, and restrict fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains.

$plugin-creator.name: $plugin-creator
$plugin-creator.description: Create and scaffold plugin directories for Codex with a required `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, optional plugin folders/files, valid manifest defaults, and personal-marketplace entries by default. Use when Codex needs to create a new personal plugin, add optional plugin structure, generate or update marketplace entries for plugin ordering and availability metadata, or update an existing local plugin during development with the CLI-driven cachebuster and reinstall flow.

$skill-installer.name: $skill-installer
$skill-installer.description: Install curated skills from openai/skills or other repos

src/prototypes/shuan-home-command-v3-export\annotation-source.json:113:    "prd-shuan-home-v3-export": "## PRD-蜀安首页V3单页导出

## 1. Summary

本文档说明“蜀安首页方案 A V3 单页导出”的产品需求。该页面用于把已确认的 V3 首页作为独立 Axure 导出资源交付，并在原型说明面板中提供 PRD、页面锚点和验收口径。

## 2. Contacts

| 角色 | 说明 |
|---|---|
| 业务方 | 确认页面名称、监管重点、地图聚合数量和打非治违表达是否符合汇报口径。 |
| 产品/原型评审 | 通过原型批注和 PRD 判断页面是否满足当前交付目标。 |
| 前端开发 | 根据页面锚点、文案、布局约束和验收标准完成实现或后续调整。 |

## 3. Background

蜀安首页 V3 是省级煤矿安全监管驾驶舱首页。页面需要在一屏内表达全省煤矿总体状态、区域聚合分布、日常监管重点和打非治违线索处置链路。

本次单页导出不是新增业务页面，而是把当前已收敛的 V3 首页独立出来，作为 Axure 导出资源。导出页必须保留现有业务行为、交互和视觉表现，同时补齐原型 PRD 文档入口，便于评审人员和开发人员在同一页面查看需求说明。

## 4. Objective

目标是让 V3 首页导出资源同时具备“可看、可批注、可交付”的能力。用户打开导出页后，可以查看完整首页，也可以从说明面板读取 PRD 和关键区域说明。

关键结果：

| 指标 | 验收方式 |
|---|---|
| 页面行为不变 | 首页仍展示 `command` 方案，变体切换回到 `shuan-home-command-v3`。 |
| 文档可读 | 说明面板目录中可以打开本 PRD。 |
| 锚点可定位 | 5 个核心区域均通过 `data-annotation-id` 被标注系统识别。 |
| 导出可检查 | 文件头保留 `@mode axure`，默认导出为 `Component`。 |
| 构建可验证 | 单入口构建命令执行通过。 |

## 5. Market Segment(s)

主要服务三类使用者：

| 使用者 | 任务 |
|---|---|
| 省级煤矿安全监管人员 | 快速判断全省煤矿分布、监管重点和打非治违处置态势。 |
| 原型评审人员 | 对照 PRD 和页面锚点检查页面是否符合当前评审意见。 |
| 前端开发人员 | 根据原型、锚点和验收标准完成后续开发、调整和验收。 |

约束：

- 本页只处理 V3 首页单页导出，不新增二级页面。
- 不接入真实接口、不改权限、不重绘精确行政区地图。
- 不为了通过 Axure 导出检查新增非必需 Axure API。
- 说明面板只承载交付说明，不改变页面业务组件的视觉布局。

## 6. Value Proposition(s)

- 对业务方：在同一个页面中看到首页效果和需求口径，减少截图、口头说明和页面版本之间的偏差。
- 对评审方：通过锚点直接定位顶部、左侧概况、地图、日常监管、打非治违等关键区域，反馈更准确。
- 对开发方：PRD 明确页面范围、数据口径、非范围和验收标准，减少二次理解成本。

## 7. Solution

### 7.1 UX / Prototype

页面路由：`shuan-home-command-v3`

导出资源：`shuan-home-command-v3-export`

页面保持当前 V3 首页体验：

- 顶部显示系统名称、区域选择、视频入口、报警消息、用户和设置入口。
- 左侧展示煤矿生产状态、生产情况、从业人员、数据汇聚、瓦斯灾害和水害信息。
- 中部展示四川地图及 12 个市州煤矿聚合点。
- 右侧展示日常监管和打非治违两组业务信息。
- 原型说明面板挂载 PRD 文档和关键区域锚点。

### 7.2 Key Features

#### 顶部系统栏

- 系统名称显示为：`蜀·安煤矿多源异构监测系统`。
- 视频入口以图标方式放在报警消息入口附近。
- 不新增说明文字，避免破坏顶部信息密度。

#### 左侧煤矿概况

左侧模块顺序为：

1. 煤矿生产状态统计。
2. 煤矿生产情况。
3. 从业人员情况。
4. 数据汇聚情况。
5. 煤矿瓦斯灾害情况、煤矿水害情况。

瓦斯灾害和水害作为自然灾害类补充信息，固定在左侧底部。

#### 中部地图煤矿聚合

地图按市州展示煤矿数量：

| 市州 | 数量 |
|---|---:|
| 达州市 | 41 |
| 宜宾市 | 30 |
| 攀枝花市 | 18 |
| 乐山市 | 15 |
| 广安市 | 14 |
| 泸州市 | 13 |
| 广元市 | 11 |
| 雅安市 | 10 |
| 内江市 | 9 |
| 自贡市 | 7 |
| 凉山州 | 4 |
| 巴中市 | 2 |

地图点位表达区域级聚合位置，不作为精确经纬度定位。

#### 日常监管

- “危险作业报备 / 风险分级管控 / 隐患排查”为主业务入口。
- 主业务入口展示在“证照到期提醒 / 重大灾害提醒”之前。
- 风险分级管控卡片显示“高风险 38”和“动态 7”。
- 提醒类信息保持轻量样式。

#### 打非治违

模块保留三层结构：

1. 五类智能算法线索：瓦斯、风、人、煤、电。
2. 三类专项整治：隐蔽工作面专项整治、监控系统造假专项整治、隐瞒入井人数专项整治。
3. 分级处置任务：煤矿整改、县级走访、市级研判、省挂牌督办。

不再展示“综合分析 / 多源线索交叉验证”中间行和对应连接线。

### 7.3 Technology

- 页面组件复用 `system-replica-base/pages/ShuanHomeCommandV3`。
- 导出入口保持 `function Component()` 和 `export default Component`。
- 原型说明使用 `@axhub/annotation` 的 `AnnotationViewer`。
- PRD 文档以 Markdown 文件维护，通过 `?raw` 读取后写入说明面板目录。
- 不新增 `forwardRef`、`useImperativeHandle` 或其他非必需 Axure API。

### 7.4 Assumptions

- 当前 V3 首页视觉和交互已作为本次导出的基线。
- 说明面板的浮层入口属于原型交付能力，不改变业务页面主体布局。
- 后续如果需要真实接口、二级页面或权限控制，应作为新迭代处理。

## 8. Release

首版交付：

- 保留 V3 首页当前视觉、交互和业务行为。
- 在导出页挂载原型说明面板。
- 在说明面板目录中加入本 PRD。
- 保留 5 个核心区域锚点：

| 锚点 | 对应区域 |
|---|---|
| `shuan-v3-header` | 顶部系统标题和工具入口 |
| `shuan-v3-left-overview` | 左侧煤矿概况模块组 |
| `shuan-v3-map-aggregation` | 中部四川地图煤矿聚合点 |
| `shuan-v3-daily-regulation` | 右侧日常监管模块 |
| `shuan-v3-illegal-treatment` | 右侧打非治违模块 |

后续版本：

- 接入真实地图边界和经纬度点位。
- 补充二级页面跳转和权限控制。
- 根据评审反馈继续完善锚点说明和模块级验收规则。

验收标准：

- 页面顶部显示“蜀·安煤矿多源异构监测系统”。
- 地图显示 12 个市州煤矿聚合点，数量与本文档一致。
- 日常监管中“风险分级管控”卡片显示“高风险 38”。
- 打非治违模块不出现“综合分析 / 多源线索交叉验证”。
- 说明面板目录可以打开 `PRD-蜀安首页V3单页导出`。
- `shuan-v3-*` 核心锚点可以被标注系统定位。
"

$pdf:pdf.description: Read, create, inspect, render, and verify PDF files where visual layout matters. Use Poppler rendering plus Python tools such as reportlab, pdfplumber, and pypdf for generation and extraction.

$pm-ai-shipping:intended-vs-implemented.name: $pm-ai-shipping:intended-vs-implemented
$pm-ai-shipping:intended-vs-implemented.description: The method for finding the gap between what a system is supposed to do and what the code actually does — the class of bug generic scanners miss because they have no model of intent. Defines what counts as documented intent, what counts as implementation evidence, which mismatches matter, and how to avoid hand-wavy findings. Use when auditing AI-built code, reviewing access control against documented permissions, or checking whether a codebase matches its own documentation.

$pm-ai-shipping:shipping-artifacts.name: $pm-ai-shipping:shipping-artifacts
$pm-ai-shipping:shipping-artifacts.description: The durable documentation set that makes an AI-built (vibe-coded) app reviewable before shipping. A small core every app needs — architecture, user/permission flows, permissions, variables/secrets, and a test-coverage map — plus conditional docs added only when they apply: emails, scheduled work, SEO, and embedded agents/automation. Defines what each doc must capture and how a reviewer or auditor uses it. Use when documenting a codebase for handoff, mapping user journeys and trust-boundary crossings, planning test coverage, or preparing for a security or performance audit.

$pm-execution:brainstorm-okrs.name: $pm-execution:brainstorm-okrs
$pm-execution:brainstorm-okrs.description: Brainstorm team-level OKRs aligned with company objectives — qualitative objectives with measurable key results. Use when setting quarterly OKRs, aligning team goals with company strategy, drafting objectives, or learning how to write effective OKRs.

$pm-execution:create-prd.name: $pm-execution:create-prd
$pm-execution:create-prd.description: Create a Product Requirements Document using a comprehensive 8-section template covering problem, objectives, segments, value propositions, solution, and release planning. Use when writing a PRD, documenting product requirements, preparing a feature spec, or reviewing an existing PRD.

$pm-execution:dummy-dataset.name: $pm-execution:dummy-dataset
$pm-execution:dummy-dataset.description: Generate realistic dummy datasets for testing with customizable columns, constraints, and output formats (CSV, JSON, SQL, Python script). Use when creating test data, building mock datasets, or generating sample data for development and demos.

$pm-execution:job-stories.name: $pm-execution:job-stories
$pm-execution:job-stories.description: Create job stories using the 'When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [outcome]' format with detailed acceptance criteria. Use when writing job stories, creating JTBD-style backlog items, or expressing user situations and motivations.

$pm-execution:outcome-roadmap.name: $pm-execution:outcome-roadmap
$pm-execution:outcome-roadmap.description: Transform an output-focused roadmap into an outcome-focused one that communicates strategic intent. Rewrites initiatives as outcome statements reflecting user and business impacts. Use when shifting to outcome roadmaps, making a roadmap more strategic, or rewriting feature lists as outcomes.

$pm-execution:pre-mortem.name: $pm-execution:pre-mortem
$pm-execution:pre-mortem.description: Run a pre-mortem risk analysis on a PRD or launch plan. Categorizes risks as Tigers (real problems), Paper Tigers (overblown concerns), and Elephants (unspoken worries), then classifies as launch-blocking, fast-follow, or track. Use when preparing for launch, stress-testing a product plan, or identifying what could go wrong.

$pm-execution:prioritization-frameworks.name: $pm-execution:prioritization-frameworks
$pm-execution:prioritization-frameworks.description: Reference guide to 9 prioritization frameworks with formulas, when-to-use guidance, and templates — RICE, ICE, Kano, MoSCoW, Opportunity Score, and more. Use when selecting a prioritization method, comparing frameworks like RICE vs ICE, or learning how different prioritization approaches work.

$pm-execution:release-notes.name: $pm-execution:release-notes
$pm-execution:release-notes.description: Generate user-facing release notes from tickets, PRDs, or changelogs. Creates clear, engaging summaries organized by category (new features, improvements, fixes). Use when writing release notes, creating changelogs, announcing product updates, or summarizing what shipped.

$pm-execution:retro.name: $pm-execution:retro
$pm-execution:retro.description: Facilitate a structured sprint retrospective — what went well, what didn't, and prioritized action items with owners and deadlines. Use when running a retrospective, reflecting on a sprint, creating action items from team feedback, or learning how to run effective retros.

$pm-execution:sprint-plan.name: $pm-execution:sprint-plan
$pm-execution:sprint-plan.description: Plan a sprint with capacity estimation, story selection, dependency mapping, and risk identification. Use when preparing for sprint planning, estimating team capacity, selecting stories, or balancing sprint scope against velocity.

$pm-execution:stakeholder-map.name: $pm-execution:stakeholder-map
$pm-execution:stakeholder-map.description: Build a stakeholder map using a power/interest grid, identify communication strategies per quadrant, and generate a communication plan. Use when managing stakeholders, preparing for a launch, aligning cross-functional teams, or planning stakeholder engagement.

$pm-execution:strategy-red-team.name: $pm-execution:strategy-red-team
$pm-execution:strategy-red-team.description: Red-team a PRD, roadmap, or strategy by attacking its load-bearing assumptions before reality does. Steelmans then attacks each claim, ranks failure modes by impact × likelihood × cheapness-to-test, and returns the cheapest test and kill criteria for each. Use when stress-testing a plan, pressure-testing a strategy, challenging assumptions, or preparing a doc for executive review.

$pm-execution:summarize-meeting.name: $pm-execution:summarize-meeting
$pm-execution:summarize-meeting.description: Summarize a meeting transcript into structured notes with date, participants, topic, key decisions, summary points, and action items. Use when processing meeting recordings, creating meeting notes, writing meeting minutes, or recapping discussions.

$pm-execution:test-scenarios.name: $pm-execution:test-scenarios
$pm-execution:test-scenarios.description: Create comprehensive test scenarios from user stories with test objectives, starting conditions, user roles, step-by-step actions, and expected outcomes. Use when writing QA test cases, creating test plans, defining acceptance tests, or preparing for feature validation.

$pm-execution:user-stories.name: $pm-execution:user-stories
$pm-execution:user-stories.description: Create user stories following the 3 C's (Card, Conversation, Confirmation) and INVEST criteria with descriptions, design links, and acceptance criteria. Use when writing user stories, breaking down features into backlog items, or defining acceptance criteria.

$pm-execution:wwas.name: $pm-execution:wwas
$pm-execution:wwas.description: Create product backlog items in Why-What-Acceptance format — independent, valuable, testable items with strategic context. Use when writing structured backlog items, breaking features into work items, or using the WWA format.

$pm-go-to-market:beachhead-segment.name: $pm-go-to-market:beachhead-segment
$pm-go-to-market:beachhead-segment.description: Identify the first beachhead market segment for a product launch. Evaluates segments against burning pain, willingness to pay, winnable market share, and referral potential. Use when choosing a first market, targeting an initial customer segment, or planning market entry strategy.

$pm-go-to-market:competitive-battlecard.name: $pm-go-to-market:competitive-battlecard
$pm-go-to-market:competitive-battlecard.description: Create sales-ready competitive battlecards comparing your product against a specific competitor — positioning, feature comparison, objection handling, and win/loss patterns. Use when preparing sales teams, creating competitive materials, or responding to 'why not competitor X?'

$pm-go-to-market:growth-loops.name: $pm-go-to-market:growth-loops
$pm-go-to-market:growth-loops.description: Identify growth loops (flywheels) for sustainable traction. Evaluates 5 loop types: Viral, Usage, Collaboration, User-Generated, and Referral. Use when designing growth mechanisms, building product-led traction, or understanding how growth loops work.

$pm-go-to-market:gtm-motions.name: $pm-go-to-market:gtm-motions
$pm-go-to-market:gtm-motions.description: Identify the best GTM motions and tools across 7 motion types: Inbound, Outbound, Paid Digital, Community, Partners, ABM, and PLG. Use when selecting marketing channels, choosing between inbound and outbound strategy, or planning cross-channel campaigns.

$pm-go-to-market:gtm-strategy.name: $pm-go-to-market:gtm-strategy
$pm-go-to-market:gtm-strategy.description: Create a go-to-market strategy covering marketing channels, messaging, success metrics, and launch timeline. Use when planning a product launch, creating a GTM plan from scratch, or defining a launch strategy for a new market.

$pm-go-to-market:ideal-customer-profile.name: $pm-go-to-market:ideal-customer-profile
$pm-go-to-market:ideal-customer-profile.description: Identify the Ideal Customer Profile (ICP) from research data with demographics, behaviors, JTBD, and needs. Use when defining your ICP, analyzing PMF survey data, or understanding who your best customers are.

$pm-marketing-growth:marketing-ideas.name: $pm-marketing-growth:marketing-ideas
$pm-marketing-growth:marketing-ideas.description: Generate 5 creative, cost-effective marketing ideas with channels, messaging, and engagement rationale. Use when brainstorming marketing campaigns, planning product promotion, or looking for creative marketing tactics.

$pm-marketing-growth:north-star-metric.name: $pm-marketing-growth:north-star-metric
$pm-marketing-growth:north-star-metric.description: Define a North Star Metric and 3-5 supporting input metrics that form a metrics constellation. Classify the business game (Attention, Transaction, Productivity) and validate against 7 criteria for an effective North Star. Use when choosing a North Star Metric, setting up a metrics framework, learning about the North Star Framework, or deciding what to measure.

$pm-marketing-growth:positioning-ideas.name: $pm-marketing-growth:positioning-ideas
$pm-marketing-growth:positioning-ideas.description: Brainstorm product positioning ideas differentiated from competitors. Identifies top competitors and generates positioning statements with rationale. Use when developing product positioning, differentiating from competitors, or crafting brand positioning strategy.

$pm-marketing-growth:product-name.name: $pm-marketing-growth:product-name
$pm-marketing-growth:product-name.description: Brainstorm 5 unique, memorable product names with rationale aligned to brand values and target audience. Use when naming a new product, rebranding, or exploring product name ideas.

$pm-marketing-growth:value-prop-statemen.name: $pm-marketing-growth:value-prop-statements
$pm-marketing-growth:value-prop-statemen.description: Generate value proposition statements for marketing, sales, and onboarding from existing value propositions. Use when writing marketing copy, creating sales messaging, or crafting onboarding messages.

$pm-product-discovery:analyze-feature-re.name: $pm-product-discovery:analyze-feature-requests
$pm-product-discovery:analyze-feature-re.description: Analyze and prioritize a list of feature requests by theme, strategic alignment, impact, effort, and risk. Use when reviewing customer feature requests, triaging a backlog, or making prioritization decisions.

$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-experim.name: $pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-experiments-existing
$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-experim.description: Design experiments to test assumptions for an existing product — prototypes, A/B tests, spikes, and other low-effort validation methods. Use when validating assumptions, testing feature ideas cheaply, or planning product experiments.

$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-experim.name: $pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-experiments-new
$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-experim.description: Design lean startup experiments (pretotypes) for a new product. Creates XYZ hypotheses and suggests low-effort validation methods like landing pages, explainer videos, and pre-orders. Use when validating a new product idea, creating pretotypes, or testing market demand.

$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-ideas-e.name: $pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-ideas-existing
$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-ideas-e.description: Brainstorm product ideas for an existing product using multi-perspective ideation from PM, Designer, and Engineer viewpoints. Use when generating new feature ideas, brainstorming solutions for an identified opportunity, or ideating with a product trio.

$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-ideas-n.name: $pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-ideas-new
$pm-product-discovery:brainstorm-ideas-n.description: Brainstorm feature ideas for a new product in initial discovery from PM, Designer, and Engineer perspectives. Use when starting product discovery for a new product, exploring features for a startup idea, or doing initial ideation.

$pm-product-discovery:identify-assumptio.name: $pm-product-discovery:identify-assumptions-existing
$pm-product-discovery:identify-assumptio.description: Identify risky assumptions for a feature idea in an existing product across Value, Usability, Viability, and Feasibility. Uses multi-perspective devil's advocate thinking. Use when stress-testing a feature idea, doing risk assessment, or preparing for assumption mapping.

$pm-product-discovery:identify-assumptio.name: $pm-product-discovery:identify-assumptions-new
$pm-product-discovery:identify-assumptio.description: Identify risky assumptions for a new product idea across 8 risk categories including Go-to-Market, Strategy, and Team. Use when evaluating startup risks, assessing a new product concept, or mapping assumptions for a new venture.

$pm-product-discovery:interview-script.name: $pm-product-discovery:interview-script
$pm-product-discovery:interview-script.description: Create a structured customer interview script with JTBD probing questions, warm-up, core exploration, and wrap-up sections. Follows The Mom Test principles — no leading questions, no pitching, focus on past behavior. Use when preparing for user interviews, creating interview guides, or planning discovery research.

$pm-product-discovery:metrics-dashboard.name: $pm-product-discovery:metrics-dashboard
$pm-product-discovery:metrics-dashboard.description: Define and design a product metrics dashboard with key metrics, data sources, visualization types, and alert thresholds. Use when creating a metrics dashboard, defining KPIs, setting up product analytics, or building a data monitoring plan.

$pm-product-discovery:opportunity-soluti.name: $pm-product-discovery:opportunity-solution-tree
$pm-product-discovery:opportunity-soluti.description: Build an Opportunity Solution Tree (OST) to structure product discovery — map a desired outcome to opportunities, solutions, and experiments. Based on Teresa Torres' Continuous Discovery Habits. Use when structuring discovery work, mapping opportunities to solutions, or deciding what to build next.

$pm-product-discovery:prioritize-assumpt.name: $pm-product-discovery:prioritize-assumptions
$pm-product-discovery:prioritize-assumpt.description: Prioritize assumptions using an Impact × Risk matrix and suggest experiments for each. Use when triaging a list of assumptions, deciding what to test first, or applying the assumption prioritization canvas.

$pm-product-discovery:prioritize-feature.name: $pm-product-discovery:prioritize-features
$pm-product-discovery:prioritize-feature.description: Prioritize a backlog of feature ideas based on impact, effort, risk, and strategic alignment with top 5 recommendations. Use when prioritizing a feature backlog, making scope decisions, or ranking product ideas.

$pm-product-discovery:summarize-intervie.name: $pm-product-discovery:summarize-interview
$pm-product-discovery:summarize-intervie.description: Summarize a customer interview transcript into a structured template with JTBD, satisfaction signals, and action items. Use when processing interview recordings or transcripts, synthesizing discovery interviews, or creating interview summaries.

$pm-product-strategy:ansoff-matrix.name: $pm-product-strategy:ansoff-matrix
$pm-product-strategy:ansoff-matrix.description: Generate an Ansoff Matrix analysis mapping growth strategies across market penetration, market development, product development, and diversification. Use when considering growth options, planning market expansion, or evaluating strategic growth paths.

$pm-product-strategy:business-model.name: $pm-product-strategy:business-model
$pm-product-strategy:business-model.description: Generate a Business Model Canvas with all 9 building blocks. Use when creating a business model, documenting how a business creates value, or analyzing an existing business model.

$pm-product-strategy:lean-canvas.name: $pm-product-strategy:lean-canvas
$pm-product-strategy:lean-canvas.description: Generate a Lean Canvas with problem, solution, metrics, cost structure, UVP, unfair advantage, channels, segments, and revenue. Use when exploring a lean startup canvas, testing a business hypothesis, or modeling a new venture.

$pm-product-strategy:monetization-strate.name: $pm-product-strategy:monetization-strategy
$pm-product-strategy:monetization-strate.description: Brainstorm 3-5 monetization strategies with audience fit, risks, and validation experiments. Use when exploring revenue models, evaluating pricing strategies, or deciding how to monetize a product.

$pm-product-strategy:pestle-analysis.name: $pm-product-strategy:pestle-analysis
$pm-product-strategy:pestle-analysis.description: Perform a PESTLE analysis covering Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, and Environmental factors. Use when assessing the macro environment, doing strategic planning, or evaluating external factors affecting your business.

$pm-product-strategy:porters-five-forces.name: $pm-product-strategy:porters-five-forces
$pm-product-strategy:porters-five-forces.description: Perform Porter's Five Forces analysis — competitive rivalry, supplier power, buyer power, threat of substitutes, and threat of new entrants. Use when analyzing industry dynamics, assessing competitive forces, or evaluating market attractiveness.

$pm-product-strategy:pricing-strategy.name: $pm-product-strategy:pricing-strategy
$pm-product-strategy:pricing-strategy.description: Analyze and design pricing strategies including pricing models, competitive pricing analysis, willingness-to-pay estimation, and price elasticity. Use when setting prices, evaluating pricing models, preparing for a pricing change, or comparing freemium vs paid approaches.

$pm-product-strategy:product-strategy.name: $pm-product-strategy:product-strategy
$pm-product-strategy:product-strategy.description: Create a comprehensive product strategy using the 9-section Product Strategy Canvas — vision, segments, costs, value propositions, trade-offs, metrics, growth, capabilities, and defensibility. Use when building a product strategy, creating a strategic plan, or defining product direction.

$pm-product-strategy:product-vision.name: $pm-product-strategy:product-vision
$pm-product-strategy:product-vision.description: Brainstorm an inspiring, achievable, and emotional product vision that motivates teams and aligns stakeholders. Use when defining or refining a product vision, creating a vision statement, or aligning the team around a shared direction.

$pm-product-strategy:startup-canvas.name: $pm-product-strategy:startup-canvas
$pm-product-strategy:startup-canvas.description: Generate a Startup Canvas combining Product Strategy (9 sections) and Business Model (costs + revenue) for a new product. An alternative to BMC and Lean Canvas that separates strategy from business model. Use when launching a new product or evaluating a startup concept.

$pm-product-strategy:swot-analysis.name: $pm-product-strategy:swot-analysis
$pm-product-strategy:swot-analysis.description: Perform a detailed SWOT analysis — strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats with actionable recommendations. Use when doing strategic assessment, competitive analysis, or evaluating a product or business position.

$pm-product-strategy:value-proposition.name: $pm-product-strategy:value-proposition
$pm-product-strategy:value-proposition.description: Design a detailed value proposition using a 6-part JTBD template — Who, Why, What before, How, What after, Alternatives. Use when creating a value proposition, analyzing customer value delivery, or articulating why customers should choose your product.

$presentations:Presentations.name: $presentations:Presentations
$presentations:Presentations.description: Create or edit PowerPoint or Google Slides decks

$product-design:audit.name: $product-design:audit
$product-design:audit.description: Audit or critique a product flow, journey, workflow, funnel, onboarding path, checkout path, settings path, screen, or multi-step product experience by capturing screenshots first, then reporting UX, design, and accessibility findings inline from that evidence. Use Figma only when the user explicitly asks for a board. Use when the user asks to audit, review, critique, inspect, assess, analyze, evaluate, or give feedback on a product experience.

$product-design:design-qa.name: $product-design:design-qa
$product-design:design-qa.description: Internal prototype QA helper. Use only after a Product Design prototype, URL-to-code build, or image-to-code build has a source visual target and a rendered implementation to compare before handoff. Do not use for broad UX critique, design critique, product audits, or flow reviews; route those user-facing requests to audit.

$product-design:get-context.name: $product-design:get-context
$product-design:get-context.description: Mandatory design-brief gate for clarifying the product and outcome. Use before ideation, image-to-code builds, redesigns, or product UI work to clarify missing product information and play back the brief before proceeding.

$product-design:ideate.name: $product-design:ideate
$product-design:ideate.description: Generate image-based alternatives, remixes, or new design directions from a Product Design brief. Use when the user asks for design variants, visual exploration, remixes, or image-generated approaches from provided context.

$product-design:image-to-code.name: $product-design:image-to-code
$product-design:image-to-code.description: Implement a selected image, screenshot, mockup, or Image Gen reference as a faithful, responsive frontend.

$product-design:index.name: $product-design:index
$product-design:index.description: Use when Product Design is explicitly invoked, or when the user's main goal is to explore a design, research UX, audit or critique a flow, faithfully clone a visual source, check a built design, or share a prototype. Do not use Product Design for ordinary implementation unless the user explicitly asks for it.

$product-design:research.name: $product-design:research
$product-design:research.description: Run fast, source-grounded UX research on the highest-signal problems users are experiencing with a user-specified digital product. Use when the user asks to research user pain, UX friction, onboarding issues, docs/help problems, developer experience friction, support pain, product workflow issues, or current user complaints for a named product.

$product-design:share.name: $product-design:share
$product-design:share.description: Share a runnable prototype using the user's preferred deployment tool.

$product-design:url-to-code.name: $product-design:url-to-code
$product-design:url-to-code.description: Clone a live URL as a runnable frontend-only local app.

$product-design:user-context.name: $product-design:user-context
$product-design:user-context.description: Load or manage Product Design's saved user context. Use when the user asks to set up Product Design, get started, onboard, save product or design sources, see what Product Design remembers, update saved context, or remember Product Design preferences. Examples include product URLs, Figma files, screenshots, reference images, codebase paths, Storybook, tokens, design systems, brand assets, and general product/design notes.

$sites:sites-building.name: $sites:sites-building
$sites:sites-building.description: Use Sites to build websites, including landing pages, portfolios, dashboards, portals, trackers, hubs, and internal tools. Always use Sites when the project contains `.openai/hosting.json`.

$sites:sites-hosting.name: $sites:sites-hosting
$sites:sites-hosting.description: Host websites with Sites. Always use after `sites-building`, and use for website publishing, deployment, hosting management, or projects containing `.openai/hosting.json`.

$spreadsheets:Spreadsheets.name: $spreadsheets:Spreadsheets
$spreadsheets:Spreadsheets.description: Use this skill when a user requests to create, modify, analyze, visualize, or work with spreadsheet files (`.xlsx`, `.xls`, `.csv`, `.tsv`) or Google Sheets-targeted spreadsheet artifacts with formulas, formatting, charts, tables, and recalculation.

$superpowers:brainstorming.name: $superpowers:brainstorming
$superpowers:brainstorming.description: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.

$superpowers:dispatching-parallel-agents.name: $superpowers:dispatching-parallel-agents
$superpowers:dispatching-parallel-agents.description: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies

$superpowers:executing-plans.name: $superpowers:executing-plans
$superpowers:executing-plans.description: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints

$superpowers:finishing-a-development-bra.name: $superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch
$superpowers:finishing-a-development-bra.description: Use when implementation is complete, all tests pass, and you need to decide how to integrate the work - guides completion of development work by presenting structured options for merge, PR, or cleanup

$superpowers:receiving-code-review.name: $superpowers:receiving-code-review
$superpowers:receiving-code-review.description: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation

$superpowers:requesting-code-review.name: $superpowers:requesting-code-review
$superpowers:requesting-code-review.description: Use when completing tasks, implementing major features, or before merging to verify work meets requirements

$superpowers:subagent-driven-development.name: $superpowers:subagent-driven-development
$superpowers:subagent-driven-development.description: Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session

$superpowers:systematic-debugging.name: $superpowers:systematic-debugging
$superpowers:systematic-debugging.description: Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes

$superpowers:test-driven-development.name: $superpowers:test-driven-development
$superpowers:test-driven-development.description: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code

$superpowers:using-git-worktrees.name: $superpowers:using-git-worktrees
$superpowers:using-git-worktrees.description: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - ensures an isolated workspace exists via native tools or git worktree fallback

$superpowers:using-superpowers.name: $superpowers:using-superpowers
$superpowers:using-superpowers.description: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions

$superpowers:verification-before-complet.name: $superpowers:verification-before-completion
$superpowers:verification-before-complet.description: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always

$superpowers:writing-plans.name: $superpowers:writing-plans
$superpowers:writing-plans.description: Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code

$superpowers:writing-skills.name: $superpowers:writing-skills
$superpowers:writing-skills.description: Use when creating new skills, editing existing skills, or verifying skills work before deployment

$template-creator:template-creator.name: $template-creator:template-creator
$template-creator:template-creator.description: Create or update a reusable personal Codex artifact-template skill. Use when the user invokes $template-creator or asks in natural language to create a template using, from, or based on an attached Word document, PowerPoint presentation, or Excel workbook, or explicitly asks to edit or update a passed artifact-template skill. Do not use for one-off artifact creation from an existing template.

$ui-reference-to-code.name: $ui-reference-to-code
$ui-reference-to-code.description: Recreate UI reference images, screenshots, Figma exports, prototype renders, dashboards, web pages, or mobile screens as high-fidelity maintainable frontend code. Use when asked to turn a UI design or effect image into code, reproduce a screen nearly 1:1, extract only the visual assets that code cannot reliably rebuild, implement the page in an existing or new frontend target, and verify fidelity with real browser screenshots.

$visualize:visualize.name: $visualize:visualize
$visualize:visualize.description: Create visualizations and interactive tools in conversation. Use when asked to show how something works, make simulators or labs, maps, plots, charts or graphs, comparisons, scenarios, adjustable inputs, and exploration.

$imagegen.name: $imagegen
$imagegen.description: Generate or edit raster images when the task benefits from AI-created bitmap visuals such as photos, illustrations, textures, sprites, mockups, or transparent-background cutouts. Use when Codex should create a brand-new image, transform an existing image, or derive visual variants from references, and the output should be a bitmap asset rather than repo-native code or vector. Do not use when the task is better handled by editing existing SVG/vector/code-native assets, extending an established icon or logo system, or building the visual directly in HTML/CSS/canvas.

$openai-docs.name: $openai-docs
$openai-docs.description: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs, asks about Codex itself or choosing Codex surfaces, needs up-to-date official documentation with citations, help choosing the latest model for a use case, or model upgrade and prompt-upgrade guidance; use OpenAI docs MCP tools for non-Codex docs questions, use the Codex manual helper first for broad Codex self-knowledge, and restrict fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains.

$plugin-creator.name: $plugin-creator
$plugin-creator.description: Create and scaffold plugin directories for Codex with a required `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, optional plugin folders/files, valid manifest defaults, and personal-marketplace entries by default. Use when Codex needs to create a new personal plugin, add optional plugin structure, generate or update marketplace entries for plugin ordering and availability metadata, or update an existing local plugin during development with the CLI-driven cachebuster and reinstall flow.

$skill-installer.name: $skill-installer
$skill-installer.description: Install curated skills from openai/skills or other repos

parts.text:
Warning: Skill descriptions were shortened to fit the 2% skills context budget. Codex can still see every skill, but some descriptions are shorter. Disable unused skills or plugins to leave more room for the rest.

这次是基于页面批注做定点修改，我先查相关技能说明和仓库里对应页面的实现位置，确认改动只落在指定页面和元素上。

> - 打非治违, 首页, 业务系统首页, 业务工作台, 线索办理工作台, 闭环台账首页, 算法研判门户, 方案图, Product Design, get-context, ideate, ShuanHomeCommandV3.tsx,
 ShuanDrilldownPages.tsx
  
  ## User preferences
> - The prototype anchors for this topic are already in the repo: `src/prototypes/system-replica-base/pages/ShuanHomeCo
mmandV3.tsx`, `src/prototypes/system-replica-base/pages/ShuanHomeConcepts.tsx`, `src/prototypes/system-replica-base/pag
es/shuan-drilldowns/ShuanDrilldownPages.tsx`, and `src/prototypes/system-replica-base/pages/shuan-drilldowns/data.ts` [
Task 1]
  - The validated framing for this rollout was `线索办理工作台 + 任务闭环台账 + 轻量研判能力`, with the homepage acting as a business work
flow hub rather than a large dashboard [Task 1]
  - The design-space names that were concrete enough to reuse are `线索办理工作台`, `闭环台账首页`, and `算法研判门户` [Task 1]
> - browser comment, selected node, screenshot, comment text, daily-regulation-rate-ring, shuan-home-command-v3, ShuanH
omeCommandV3.tsx, ShuanHomeConcepts.tsx, system-replica-base, style.css, CSS override, targeted verification
  
  ## User preferences
> - when the user first asks only to locate the page and the concrete requirement has not arrived yet, stop at locating
 `src/prototypes/system-replica-base/index.tsx`, `pages/ShuanHomeCommandV3.tsx`, and `style.css` instead of inventing e
dits or broader analysis [Task 1]
  - when handling a very specific browser comment and the selected node, screenshot, and comment text are already provi
ded, default to a narrow CSS tweak path instead of broad repo/context rediscovery [Task 1]
  
> - For `shuan-home-command-v3` / `system-replica-base` homepage tweaks, the practical edit surface is the page compone
nt plus the layered shared stylesheet: `src/prototypes/system-replica-base/pages/ShuanHomeCommandV3.tsx` or `ShuanHomeC
oncepts.tsx`, and `src/prototypes/system-replica-base/style.css` [Task 1]
  - The faster comment-handling path is: read the selector/class from the browser comment, patch the local V3 override 
near the relevant widget such as `daily-regulation-rate-ring`, then verify the same viewport with the current prototype
 entry and screenshot rather than reopening broad project discovery [Task 1]
  - The Shuan homepage styles are heavily layered, so appending a more specific override near the end of `style.css` is
 safer than rewriting older shared rules in place [Task 1]
> - 蜀安首页 V3, 设计思路, 汇报稿, .spec/docs, PRD, open-design-breakdown, ShuanHomeCommandV3.tsx, ShuanHomeConcepts.tsx, UTF-8 ve
rification, not a git repository
  
  ## User preferences
> - The reliable source set for this kind of writeup is the actual prototype entry plus the shared system-replica pages
 and local `.spec/docs` notes: `src/prototypes/shuan-home-command-v3-export/index.tsx`, `src/prototypes/system-replica-
base/pages/ShuanHomeCommandV3.tsx`, `src/prototypes/system-replica-base/pages/ShuanHomeConcepts.tsx`, `src/prototypes/s
huan-home-command-v3-export/.spec/docs/PRD-蜀安首页功能模块说明.md`, and `src/prototypes/shuan-home-command-v3-export/.spec/open-
design-breakdown.md` [Task 1]
  - The V3 homepage framing validated in this run was a three-column cockpit: left = foundational supervision state, ce
nter = Sichuan map aggregation and risk positioning, right = daily regulation plus anti-illegal governance; the report 
positioned V3 as an upgrade from a generic entry page to a `安全监管指挥首页` [Task 1]
  - The saved report path is a stable retrieval handle for future edits: `src/prototypes/shuan-home-command-v3-export/.
spec/docs/蜀安首页V3设计思路汇报稿.md` [Task 1]
> - shuan-home-command-v3-major-hazard-reminder, mhr-map-btn, `鈼?`, src/prototypes/shuan-home-command-v3-export/index.t
sx, unsupported call, mcp__open_design__get_file, mcp__node_repl__js, CreateProcessWithLogonW failed: 5
  
  ## Task 2: Change the coal-mine unit wording in `RegulatedMinePanel`
> - ShuanHomeCommandV3.tsx, RegulatedMinePanel, coal-mine stats, `<em>澶?/em>`, `<em>瀹?/em>`, Select-String, Panel, dire
ct execution, startup acknowledgement
  
  ## Task 3: Apply multi-file annotation-driven UI edits across home V3 and drilldowns
> - annotation-driven edit, ShuanHomeCommandV3.tsx, DangerousWorkReportPage.tsx, ShuanDrilldownPages.tsx, drill-dangero
us-map-legend, reset button, mhr-map-controls, `鈼?`, plain-language summary
  
  ## Task 4: Locate the Shuan annotation targets when the design-search tool is unavailable
> - mcp__open_design__search_files, regulated-mine-panel, drill-dangerous-map-panel, Get-ChildItem -Recurse -Filter *.t
sx, Select-String, ShuanHomeCommandV3.tsx:304-310, DangerousWorkReportPage.tsx:298, ShuanDrilldownPages.tsx:510
  
  ## User preferences
> - In this repo, the export wrapper `src/prototypes/shuan-home-command-v3-export/index.tsx` is often only the entrypoi
nt; the actual edit surface for annotated Shuan homepage content may be in `src/prototypes/system-replica-base/pages/Sh
uanHomeCommandV3.tsx` and the linked drilldown pages under `src/prototypes/system-replica-base/pages/shuan-drilldowns/`
 [Task 2][Task 3][Task 4]
  - `RegulatedMinePanel` is the concrete anchor for the home V3 coal-production summary. It renders through the shared 
`Panel` component, so the safe edit is usually to change its inner content/units, not to add a second custom header blo
ck [Task 2][Task 3]
> - The recurring drilldown anchors from these runs were `DangerousWorkReportPage.tsx` for `drill-dangerous-map-legend`
 and the reset button, plus `ShuanDrilldownPages.tsx` for `MajorHazardReminderPage` and the `mhr-map-controls` block co
ntaining `+`, `-`, and `鈼?` buttons [Task 3][Task 4]
> - When the design-search MCP surface is unavailable, the working repo-local search path is `Get-ChildItem -Recurse -F
ilter *.tsx | ForEach-Object { Select-String -Path $_.FullName -Pattern ... -Encoding UTF8 }`, keyed on class names and
 distinctive UI strings such as `regulated-mine-panel`, `drill-dangerous-map-panel`, `drill-dangerous-map-legend`, and 
`mhr-map-btn` [Task 3][Task 4]
> - The concrete location anchors validated in these runs were `ShuanHomeCommandV3.tsx:304-310` around `RegulatedMinePa
nel`, `DangerousWorkReportPage.tsx:298` around the map panel, and `ShuanDrilldownPages.tsx:510` for the `鈼?` control [T
ask 4]
  - Related skill: skills/dfzg-shuan-annotation-ui-edit/SKILL.md [Task 1][Task 2][Task 3][Task 4]
  
> - symptom: a Shuan homepage text change appears to belong in the export entry file but cannot be found there -> cause
: the visible UI is implemented in a shared `system-replica-base` page component -> fix: trace from the export wrapper 
into `ShuanHomeCommandV3.tsx` or the relevant drilldown page before editing [Task 2][Task 3]
  - symptom: repeated regex or broad text-replace passes corrupt JSX structure -> cause: editing around shared `Panel`/
header markup without inspecting the component shape first -> fix: inspect the surrounding JSX, prefer one small target
ed replacement, and keep the shared title wrapper intact unless the task explicitly changes it [Task 2][Task 3]
> - symptom: `Select-String -Recurse` fails or the legend/button block is hard to target by visible text alone -> cause
: the direct PowerShell pattern is brittle for nested JSX blocks -> fix: use `Get-ChildItem -Recurse` plus `Select-Stri
ng`, then remove the surrounding class-based block (`drill-dangerous-map-legend`, `mhr-map-controls`) instead of matchi
ng only rendered text [Task 3][Task 4]
  
  # Task Group: dfzg-project-shuan-demo / homepage drilldown route wiring and cross-entrypoint consistency
> - homepage drilldown checklist, route chain, page target, ShuanHomeCommandV3.tsx, .spec/docs, prototype docs, postmor
tem, entrypoint consistency
  
  ## User preferences
> - This defect was not a style/component issue; it was a registration-chain omission. `ShuanHomeCommandV3.tsx:171` alr
eady pointed to `shuan-home-command-v3-license-expiry-reminder`, but the downstream route table and dispatcher were mis
sing the route [Task 1]
  - The export entry `src/prototypes/shuan-home-command-v3-export/index.tsx` sends non-home routes through the overlay 
drilldown flow, so a missing registration can surface differently between export and `system-replica-base` even when th
e source homepage card is the same [Task 1]
  - After the fix, `data.ts` contains `shuanLicenseExpiryReminderData`, `shuanDrilldownRoutePages` includes that route,
 and `ShuanDrilldownPages.tsx` dispatches to `LicenseExpiryReminderPage`, so both entrypoints reach the same page [Task
 1]
